---
title: Bash Profiles
subtitle: "Customizing Bash (sh) terminals: Linux and GitBash on Windows"
---

::: {.callout-important}
Bash shell has been replaced with Z shell in Mac OS. The information on this page will no longer modify the Mac Terminal window. See [Zsh Profiles](zsh.qmd) for customizing Mac terminals.
:::

The appearance and behavior of Bash shell terminal windows can be configured using a series of hidden files. It is best practice to take a module approach using several separate files, all of which must be located in the user home directory (`~/`), and each of which has a unique purpose:

1. `~/.bash_profile`: Read by the Linux and Windows upon login  
2. `~/.bashrc`: Read every time a "non-login" terminal session is started. On Linux platforms, this means opening a new tab, while on Windows, it means opening a new Git Bash window. If present, this file should be read first.  
3. `~/.bash_prompt`: An optional file to configure terminal prompts (*i.e.*, the text that precedes user commands)
4. `~/.aliases`: An optional file to hold user shortcut aliases.  

These files are linked together using the `source` command. More information can be found [here](https://wiki.archlinux.org/title/Bash/Prompt_customization).

## My configurations

```{.bash filename=".bash_profile"}
# Load bashrc if it exists
if [ -f ~/.bashrc ]; then
    source ~/.bashrc
fi

```

<br>

```{.bash filename=".bashrc"}
# Prevent Python prompt hijacking
export VIRTUAL_ENV_DISABLE_PROMPT=1

# Load custom prompt if it exists
if [ -f ~/.bash_prompt ]; then
    source ~/.bash_prompt
fi

# Load shortcuts/aliases if they exist
if [ -f ~/.aliases ]; then
    source ~/.aliases
fi

```

<br>

```{.bash filename=".bash_prompt"}
generate_prompt() {
    local RS='\[\033[0m\]'             
    local TEXT='\[\033[38;5;250m\]'    
    local GREEN='\[\033[38;5;108m\]'   
    local YELLOW='\[\033[38;5;180m\]'  
    local BLUE='\[\033[38;5;110m\]'    

    local VENV=""
    if [[ -n "$VIRTUAL_ENV" ]]; then
        VENV="${TEXT}(${GREEN}$(basename "$VIRTUAL_ENV")${TEXT}) "
    fi

    local GIT=""
    local BRANCH=$(git branch --show-current 2>/dev/null)
    if [[ -n "$BRANCH" ]]; then
        GIT=" ${TEXT}[${BLUE}${BRANCH}${TEXT}]"
    fi

    PS1="${VENV}${YELLOW}\W${GIT}${TEXT} \$ ${RS}"
}

PROMPT_COMMAND=generate_prompt

```

<br>

```{.bash filename=".aliases"}
# LIST BEHAVIOR
# Cross-Platform 'ls' configuration
if [[ "$OSTYPE" == "darwin"* ]]; then
    # macOS flags: -F for indicators, -G for color
    alias ls="ls -FG"
else
    # Windows/Linux (GNU) flags: -F for indicators, --color=auto for color
    alias ls="ls -F --color=auto"
fi
alias ll="ls -lh"
alias la="ls -lha"

# INTERACTIVE REMOVE
alias rm="rm -i"
alias mv="mv -i"

# NAVIGATION
alias ..="cd .."
alias ...="cd ../.."
alias ....="cd ../../.."
alias .....="cd ../../../.."

# COMMON DIRECTORIES
alias dc="cd ~/Documents"
alias dl="cd ~/Downloads"
alias dt="cd ~/Desktop"
alias home="cd ~"

# GITHUB SHORTCUTS
alias gs="git status"
alias gc="git commit -m"
```

## Discussion

In the setup above, `.bash_prompt` configures the terminal prompt to show `(pyvenv) user@pwd.basename [git-branch] $ `, where

* `(pyvenv)` is the name of the activated Python virtual environment, if applicable (omitted if not)
* `user@pwd.basename` is the username and the current directory of the present working directory (pwd)
* `[git-branch]` is the checked out Github branch if the present working directory is a Github repository (omitted otherwise)
* ` $ ` is the final character, followed by a space, preceding user prompts.  

For aesthetical convenience, these components are colored green, yellow, blue, and light grey, respectively. User prompts are white font.

The `.aliases` controls, among other things, how files and directories are listed in the terminal:

1. **List behavior:** Shortcuts for detailed lists (`-l`) with human-readable file sizes (`-h) and including hidden files (`-a`). Items are color-coded and marked depending on type (*e.g.*,directory, file, executable file) for clarity. Some differences exist between platforms, so an operating system (OS) check is used to make the file itself OS-agnostic.
2. **Interactive remove:** Always ask before deleting (`rm`) or overwriting (`mv`, if the file already exists), a safeguard against accidentally removing files
3. **Navigation:** Shortcuts for moving up the directory chain
4. **Common directories:** Shortcuts for moving to commonly-used directories
5. **GitHub Shortcuts:** Shortcuts for commonly-used Git commands

## Understanding the Terminal Prompt

### `PS1` Letter codes

| Code | Description                                                          |
|:----:|:---------------------------------------------------------------------|
| \\a | an ASCII bell character (07) |
| \\d | date in "Weekday Month Date" format (*e.g.*, "Tue May 26") |
| \\D{format} | format is passed to strftime(3) and the result is inserted into the prompt string; an empty format results in a locale-specific time representation. The braces are required |
| \\e | an ASCII escape character (033) |
| \\h | hostname up to the first "." |
| \\H | hostname |
| \\j | number of jobs currently managed by the shell |
| \\l | basename of the shell’s terminal device name |
| \\n | newline |
| \\r | carriage return |
| \\s | name of the shell, the basename of $0 (the portion following the final slash) |
| \\t | current time in 24-hour HH:MM:SS format |
| \\T | current time in 12-hour HH:MM:SS format |
| \\@ | current time in 12-hour am/pm format |
| \\A | current time in 24-hour HH:MM format |
| \\u | username of the current user |
| \\v | version of bash (e.g., 2.00) |
| \\V | release of bash, version + patch level (e.g., 2.00.0) |
| \\w | current working directory, with $HOME abbreviated with a tilde |
| \\W | basename of the current working directory, with $HOME abbreviated with a tilde |
| \\\! | history number of this command |
| \\\# | command number of this command |
| \\\$ | if the effective UID is 0, a #, otherwise a $ |
| \\nnn | the character corresponding to the octal number nnn |
| \\\\ | a backslash |
| \\\[ | begin a sequence of non-printing characters, which could be used to embed a terminal control sequence into the prompt |
| \\\] | end a sequence of non-printing characters |
: Letter codes for `PS1` bash prompts

### Colors

Colors and text formatting syntax can be found [here](https://misc.flogisoft.com/bash/tip_colors_and_formatting). To see samples and codes of the supported colors:

```bash
for C in {0..255}; do
    tput setab $C
    echo -n "$C "
done
tput sgr0
echo
```

## After updating any of these files

After updating any these files, either restart the terminal or use `source <filename>` to load the new settings.
